xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of CDs(WTL)
Effects of pH and ionic strength on fluorescence of CDs(WTL)
The establishment of three kinds of quantitative models based on the fluorescent system of CDs(WTL)
Quantification of TC in tablet samples from three pharmaceutical companies
Quantification of TC in river water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Project of Guizhou Province (grant numbers [2020]4Y133 , [2019]1070 , and [2020]4102 ), the Science and Technology Project of China National Tobacco Corporation Guizhou Company (grant numbers 201822 and 201922 ), and the High-Level Innovative Talents Training Program of Guizhou Province (grant number 20165663 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The illustration of the synthesis of CDs(WTL) and its application to detect TC by three quantification models.
The TEM imaging (a), HRTEM imaging (b), particle size distribution (c), and FT-IR spectrum (d) of the CDs(WTL).
The XPS spectra of the CDs(WTL). The XPS survey spectrum (a) and high-resolution XPS spectra with its corresponding fitting curves of C1s (b), O1s (c), and N1s (d).

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs(WTL), insets are photos of the CDs(WTL) under sunlight (left) and irradiated by 365 nm light (right) (a) 3D fluorescence contour map of the CDs(WTL) (b).

The excitation-dependent and emission-dependent properties of the CDs(WTL). Excitation spectra recorded at different emission wavelengths (a), Emission spectra measured under different excitation wavelengths (b).
Tolerance of fluorescence signal of the CDs (WTL) to different NaCl concentrations (a) and pH values (b).
Variation of fluorescence intensity of CDs (WTL) with interference coexisted, different metal ions (a), common organic chemicals with the concentration of 200.0 μM (b).
Fluorescence intensity of the CDs(WTL) in the presence of different concentrations of TC(a), the corresponding normalized spectra (b).

Predicted concentration of TC versus the actual concentration (in calibration, validation, spiked river water samples, and spiked TCA samples), TQ model (a), PLS model (b), and QFM model (c); The average relative predictive error (ARPE) of three different methods to treat the validation, spiked water and spiked TCA samples (d).
Chromatograms of TC (30.00 μM), 3 kinds of real tablet samples of TCA, TCB, and TCC (a), Calibration curve for TC, where the dependent variable y represents the peak area (b).
Quantification results of TC in three kinds of TC tablet samples by QFM, PLS, and TQ.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SD refers to the standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the results predicted by the presented approach of the QFM method with that predicted by the HPLC reference method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
At the 5% significance level, the critical value
F 2,2
is 39.0. At the 5% significance level, the critical value for t is 2.78.

head.ce:title: Waste tobacco leaves derived carbon dots for tetracycline detection: Improving quantitative accuracy with the aid of chemometric model

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guizhou Academy of Tobacco Science, Guiyang, 550081, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Tobacco Science, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Tobacco Science, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Tobacco Quality Research of Guizhou Province, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tobacco Quality Research of Guizhou Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Tobacco Quality Research of Guizhou Province, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Tobacco Science, Guizhou University, Guiyang, 550025, China.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots) are one of the central members of carbon nanomaterials family. In general, C-dots are well-dispersed spherical-like particles with sizes less than 10 nm, which have been attracted more considerable scholarly attention in recent years with many excellent traits of low toxicity, good light resistance, good biocompatibility, high chemical inertness, and easy surface modification [1–4]. So far, C-dots have been deemed as excellent potential alternative substitute nanomaterials for semiconductor quantum dots, which have been extensively applied in the fields of fluorescence sensing [5–9], cell imaging [10–13], drug delivery [14,15], in vivo imaging [16–18], photocatalysis [19,20], multicolor light-emitting diode (LED) production [21,22], energy conversion and storage [23,24], etc. In addition, it gradually becomes one of the hot research topics in many fields.

At present, many researchers have attempted to utilize biomass wastes as raw materials for preparing carbon dots. Biomass wastes such as lemon peel [25], coffee grounds [26], etc, have been successfully utilized. The effective conversion of biomass wastes into carbon dots could not only realize resource utilization, offer organic wastes management, reduce harmfulness, even achieve multi-functional transformation, relieve the pressure of resource shortage, but also reduce environmental pollution induced by improper handling of biomass wastes. Tobacco is an economic crop used for its leaves, only the tobacco leaves that satisfy the requirements of tobacco industry processing can be effectively utilized, low-quality leaves and most of the tobacco by-products were disposed of as wastes, therefore, inducing great amounts of tobacco wastes per year [27,28]. Tobacco wastes are renewable natural biomass sources and are available abundantly. However, in reality, most of the tobacco by-products are discarded directly to the soil, landfilled, or burned optionally. Such disposal manner not only leads to a waste of resources but also causes serious environmentally problems, far from the requirement of environmental sustainable development, and even threatens human health [29]. Thus, whether from the perspective of resource reusing or from environmental protection, researches on the reutilization of tobacco leaves are of far-research significance.

Up to the present, carbon dots produced from biomass and biomass wastes have been widely used as fluorescent probes for quantitative detection of analytes of interest, and satisfactory results have been achieved. Whereas, in most of the reported papers, the quantitative determination of analytes of interest based on fluorescent systems were usually combined with the traditional univariate quantification model (TQ) based on the standard curve method, which was established between the concentration of analytes of interest and the fluorescence intensity at one wavelength [30].

The accuracy of quantitative results depends on the stability of fluorescence intensity at the selected wavelength. Whereas the fluorescence intensity is generally influenced by the intensity variation of a light source, variation of optical path length, and the existence of fluorescence interference, thus inducing low anti-interference ability of standard curve method, it is difficult for the obtained coefficient of determination (R 2) to reach 0.99 [31–34]. There are two possible reasons: (1) the existence of spectral interference. Carbon dots solution is a heterogeneous mixture system with slight fluctuations in particle size and surface groups. This heterogeneous fluorescent system inducing a mild variated of the optical path length among different measurements, which further makes the results deviate the calibration curve. (2) A slight wavelength shift occurred on the fluorescent peak. The fluorescent spectra turned red-shift as the concentration of analytes of interest increased, this phenomenon leads to unsatisfactory quantification results obtained from the traditional standard curve method based on the fluorescent intensity at one wavelength.

As analyzed above, in order to improve the quantitative accuracy and the ability of anti-interference of the quantitative model based on fluorescent signal, data of the whole spectrum can be utilized with the aid of the multivariate calibration method. Partial least squares (PLS) [35–37] is one of the most classical and commonly used multivariate analysis method, it was first proposed by H. Wold in the field of econometrics and was first used by Kowalski et al. in the field of chemistry [38]. PLS can make full use of spectral data to establish a calibration model, enhance anti-interference ability, and improve quantitative accuracy. However, for complex heterogeneous systems with fluorescent spectrum shifts, the PLS method also has some limitations. Novel chemometric method such as quantitative fluorescent model (QFM) [39,40] based on the algorithm of optical path length estimation and correction (OPLEC) [41,42] is expected for acquiring accurate results.

In this work, carbon dots from waste tobacco leaves (CDs(WTL)) have been synthesized through a facile and eco-friendly hydrothermal method. Furthermore, the structures and properties have been characterized by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), transmission electron microscope (TEM), fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, ultraviolet spectroscopy (UV–Vis), and fluorescent spectroscopy (FL). As CDs(WTL) possess a selective response to tetracycline, thus CDs(WTL) have been utilized as an efficient fluorescence probe for quantitative analysis of tetracycline in real samples of tetracycline tablet and river water. With a view to achieving satisfactory results, three different quantitative methods based on TQ, PLS, QFM were employed, and the obtained results have been compared and analyzed. In addition, in order to verify the accuracy of the results obtained from the fluorescent method combined with the chemometric model, the reference method of high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was adopted, and the corresponding results of F-test and t-test were analyzed. The process of detecting tetracycline (TC) by the prepared CDs(WTL) was shown in Fig. 1 .

Waste tobacco leaves were obtained from key laboratory of tobacco research of Guizhou province, Guizhou University (Guiyang, China). Melamine, chloramphenicol, chlortetracycline hydrochloride, tetracycline hydrochloride, oxytetracycline dihydrate, penicillin G Na, amoxicillin, ampicillin trihydrate, doxycycline hyclate, cefazolin sodium salt, quinine sulfate dihydrate, ethylenediamine monohydrate, Na2HPO4·12H2O, C6H8O7·H2O, and part of metal ions (Al3+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, K+, and Ca2+) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and the other metal ions(Li+, Ag+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+) were obtained from Beijing Enokai Technology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Tetracycline (TC) tablets (specifications: 0.25 g) were purchased from a local pharmacy (Guiyang, China). All the other reagents are analytical grade and used without purification.

All fluorescence measurements were performed on a Lumina fluorescence spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, Madison, USA). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrums were recorded by an Evolution-201 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, Shanghai, China). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained with a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (FEI, Portland, USA) at 200 kV. The elemental and functional group analysis were performed on an ESCALAB 250X X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), and Nicolet is5 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Madison, USA), separately. The pH values were measured with an E6275 pH meter (Aladdin, Shanghai, China) and heated in an electric constant temperature drying oven (DHG-10HB) (Lichen, Shanghai, China). The HPLC analysis experiment was carried out using an Ultimate 3000 high performance liquid chromatography (Thermo Fisher, Madison, USA). Using a C18 column (2.1 × 250 mm, 5 μm), with an oven temperature of 30 °C was adopted for sample separation. The absorbance was recorded at 276 nm, the mobile phase was 0.2% formic acid aqueous solution: acetonitrile: methanol = 70: 20:10, and the injection volume was 20 μL.

The CDs(WTL) were prepared from biomass waste of tobacco leaves through a simple, eco-friendly hydrothermal approach. Various factors including the additive amount of tobacco leaves, solvent type and amount, reaction temperature, and reaction time were studied for optimizing the fluorescence properties of CDs(WTL). The final optimized experimental condition is as follows: 2.00 g of waste tobacco leaves was ground to powder, and 1.25 mL of ethylenediamine was added into a 50 mL centrifuge tube, then diluted with ultrapure water to the mark. After stirred for 30 min under ultrasonic vibration at 25 °C, the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and been continuously heated at 220 °C for 12 h in a high-temperature oven. After cooling down to room temperature, the raw carbon dots were centrifuged under 4000 rpm for 15 min and further filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane to remove large particles. Finally, the target CDs(WTL) solution was obtained and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs(WTL) was measured as relative fluorescence quantum yield [43,44]. Owing to the similar maximal excitation wavelength to CDs(WTL), quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, QY = 54%) was selected as a reference substance, the QY of CDs(WTL) was calculated as follows:
(1)
Q c
=
Q s
×
F c
A s
(
η c
)
2
F s
A c
(
η s
)
2
where Q
c and Q
s refer to QY of the CDs(WTL) and quinine sulfate, respectively; F
c and F
s are the integral areas of fluorescent emission spectra of CDs(WTL) and quinine sulfate with excitation wavelength of 360 nm; A
c and A
s represent the absorbance intensity of CDs(WTL) and quinine sulfate, respectively;
η c
and
η s
account for reflective index of CDs(WTL) and quinine sulfate solution, respectively, and the ratio was usually approximated as 1. In order to minimize reabsorption effects, the absorbance intensity of CDs(WTL) and quinine sulfate solutions were all kept below 0.05 [45].

The selectivity study has been carried out by recording fluorescence emission spectra of CDs(WTL) under the same condition. Many interference metal ions (Al3+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Li+, Ag+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+) with concentration of 200.0 μM have been studied. Additionally, many organic compounds, including oxytetracycline, amoxicillin, penicillin G Na, melamine, and other similar substances with concentration of 200.0 μM also have been investigated to test their effects upon the fluorescence intensity of CDs(WTL).

As TC could effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs(WTL), thus in this work, CDs(WTL) were used as a fluorescent probe for the quantitative determination of TC. Table S1 lists the experimental design for TC detection, 10 standard solutions were designed for building calibration model, 6 validation samples were used for testing the predictive ability of the established model. Then, the quantification model was applied for TC detection in 3 real river water samples, 4 spiked river water samples, 9 real TC tablet samples (containing tablets from 3 different pharmaceutical companies), and 12 corresponding spiked tablet samples. All the TC tablets were preprocessed as follows: 5 pieces of TC tablets were taken randomly and ground into powder in a mortar. 20.00 mg of the powder was dissolved in 10 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 HCl under the ultrasonic condition at 40 °C for 30 min, then transferred the solution to a 100 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with ultrapure water. The real river water samples were collected from Huaxi river (Guiyang, China) and filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane. Real samples and the corresponding spiked samples were prepared by mixing 2 mL filtered river water, different amounts of TC working solution, and 200 μL of the CDs(WTL) working solution (prepared by diluting 350 μL of CDs(WTL) stock solution in 100 mL ultra-pure water) in a 10 mL volumetric flask, then diluted to the mark with buffer solution (Na2HPO4 - C6H8O7, pH = 4.0). The final concentration of TC in all samples are listed in Table S1.

For the fluorescence quenching system, the fluorescence data usually obey the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)) with analytes of interest in a certain concentration range through either a static or a dynamic mechanism.
(2)
F 0
− F
F
=
K
S V
c
where F 0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of maximum emission with the absence and presence of TC, the K
SV corresponds to the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and c is the concentration of TC. Quenching data are usually demonstrated as F 0/F versus concentration of analytes because F 0/F is expected to be linear in concentration.

The traditional quantification model (TQ) is based on the univariate linear model y = m x + b, in which x represents the concentration of the analyte TC and y represents the fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission peak. The standard curve is established based on the calibration set, and then the analyte concentration in each unknown sample is predicted as x unk = (y unk – b)/m.

Partial least squares (PLS) [35–37] is one of the most classical and commonly used multivariate calibration method. PLS is based on the multivariate linear model y = X
I×J
b, in which y represents the concentrations of the analyte TC in I samples, the response matrix X
I×J represents the fluorescence spectra of I samples measured on J emission wavelengths, and b represents the coefficient vector. In the calibration step, the coefficient vector is estimated as b = P (P
T
P)
−1
W Q
T . In the prediction step, the concentration vector of the analyte TC in the unknown samples is calculated as y unk = X unk b.

The quantitative fluorescent model (QFM) [39,40] is based on the algorithm of optical path length estimation and correction (OPLEC) [41,42]. In QFM, some parameters are introduced to describe the influence of noise information and irrelevant interference information on the measured fluorescent spectra. The multiplicative effect of the calibration samples can be estimated by the Optical path length estimation and correction (OPLEC) algorithm [41]. Finally, the concentrations of analyte in unknown samples can be obtained by the ratio of the predicted values of two bilinear models according to its response matrix. Details of QFM can be found in the Supplementary Material or in the above original literatures.

In order to assess the feasibility of the quantification model and the accuracy of the analytical results, we calculate the figures of merit, such as the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) [46], the average relative predictive error (ARPE). The corresponding calculation formulas are listed as below:
(3)
LOD = 3.3
s 0
(4)
LOQ = 10
s 0
(5)
ARPE =
( 1 / I )
∑
i = 1
I
(
|
c ˆ
i
−
c i
|
/
c i
)
⋅ 100 %
Where
s 0
refers to the standard error of three prediction values of blank samples. I denotes to the total amounts of prediction samples,
c ˆ
i
and
c i
correspond to the predicted and actual concentration of analyte of interest in prediction samples, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of CDs(WTL)
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 2 a) indicates that the as-prepared CDs(WTL) possess good dispersion without coagulation, and they are spherical with a mean spring diameter of 6.30 nm (Fig. 2c). Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image displays a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.2 nm (Fig. 2b), which is very close to the diffraction planes of graphitic (sp2) [47,48], this demonstrates that the CDs(WTL) have a graphite-like structure.

The FT-IR spectrum was employed to characterize the surface functional groups of the CDs(WTL). As shown in Fig. 2d, the broad absorption peak at about 3360 cm−1 suggests the existence of O−H or/and N−H stretching vibration. The peak at 2928 cm−1 corresponds to a C−H stretching vibration. The peak at 1641 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of the C=O bond, the peak at 1580 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of the C=C stretching of polycyclic aromatic, hydrocarbons and the absorption peak at 1000–1200 cm−1 is attributed to the C−O and C−O−C bond. The peaks between 1300 and 1480 cm−1 can be identified as the C−O, C−N, N−H, or/and COO− groups. These results indicate that nitrogen had been successfully doped in the CDs(WTL) and nitrogen existed simultaneously in various forms of nitrogen-containing structures.

The surface states of the CDs(WTL) were studied by XPS spectra. There exist three characteristic binding energy peaks at 285.0 eV, 400.0 eV, and 532.0 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s electrons, respectively (Fig. 3 a). The relative contents of C, N, and O elements are nearly about 63.89%, 11.44%, and 24.67%, respectively. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum can be decomposed into 4 peaks (Fig. 3b), which correspond to 284.2 eV (C−C/C=C), 285.0 eV (C−N), 285.8 eV (C−O), and 287.3 eV (C=O) [49], respectively. The O1s spectrum exhibits two characteristic peaks C=O and C−OH at 530.6 eV and 532.1 eV [50](Fig. 3c), respectively. The N1s spectrum can be decomposed into three components of 398.8 eV, 399.5 eV, and 400.8 eV, matched with pyridine C−N=C, C−N−C, and N−H [51,52] (Fig. 3d), respectively. These results indicate that nitrogen had been successfully doped in the CDs(WTL) and nitrogen existed in various forms of nitrogen-containing structures. The results of XPS analysis are in keeping with that of the FT-IR analysis, which further confirm the existence of different oxygen-containing groups and nitrogenous structure on the surface of the CDs(WTL).

Subsequently, optical properties of the CDs(WTL) were studied in depth through the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum and the fluorescence spectrum. As shown in Fig. 4 a, in the range of 310–370 nm, the obtained CDs(WTL) presented a typical broad absorption band. The absorption band around 340 nm can be attributed to n–π∗ transition of the C=O bond, which might trap the excited state energy by the oxygen-related to surface states resulting in strong emission [53,54]. The fluorescence QY of the CDs(WTL) was measured approximately to be 13.7% by the method mentioned in section 2.4. For fluorescence properties, the maximal excitation and emission wavelengths of the CDs(WTL) are 360 nm and 430 nm, respectively. The CDs(WTL) solution emits a strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation ultraviolet lamp (365 nm), and it is almost colorless under sunlight (inserted figure in Fig. 4a).

For researching the fluorescence properties of the CDs(WTL), a three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum was studied. As demonstrated in Fig. 4b, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are 360 nm and 430 nm, separately, that is to say, when the excitation wavelength is 360 nm, the maximal fluorescence signal will be obtained at an emission wavelength of 430 nm. Additionally, from Fig. 4b, one can find that the excitation-emission contour of the CDs(WTL) is an irregular ellipse, which means the acquired emission spectra are different from each other as the excitation wavelength varies. Similarly, when recorded at diverse emission wavelengths, the obtained excitation spectra are strikingly different. The excitation-dependent properties of carbon dots had been described in many references [55,56]. However, the excitation spectra recorded at diverse emission wavelengths have not been studied. Fig. 5 a demonstrates the excitation spectra recorded at diverse emission wavelengths in the range of 380–480 nm. The excitation spectra gradually turn red-shift as the recorded emission wavelength increases. The emission spectra of the CDs(WTL) under varied excitation wavelengths in the range of 300–400 nm is demonstrated in Fig. 5b. Similar to excitation spectra, the emission spectra also present a red-shift with the excitation wavelength increases from 300 nm to 400 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH and ionic strength on fluorescence of CDs(WTL)
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the protonation and deprotonation of the surface groups of carbon dots, the effects of pH and ionic strength on the fluorescence of the CDs(WTL) were evaluated. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 a, CDs(WTL) show excellent salt tolerance, even in a solution with high ionic strength (NaCl, 1 mol L−1), the fluorescence properties are hardly affected. Additionally, the effect of pH in the range of 2.2–8.0 on fluorescence intensity of the CDs(WTL) is also slight (Fig. 6b). However, the fluorescence intensity is the highest when pH 4.0, thus in this work, pH 4.0 was selected in the quantitative application using the CDs(WTL) as a fluorescence probe.

The selectivity study of the CDs(WTL) has been carried out by measuring its fluorescence intensity with different metal ions and some common organic chemicals coexisted. As shown in Fig. 7 a, even at high concentration level of 200.0 μM, metal ions consisting of Ni2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Na+, Li+, Ag+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ nearly have no effect on fluorescence intensity of the CDs(WTL), Hg2+ have a little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs(WTL). In addition, some organic compounds (tetracyclines, chloramphenicol, oxytetracycline, and doxycycline) demonstrate a strong quenching ability to the fluorescence intensity of the CDs(WTL). In contrast, some other similar substances display no obvious effects (Fig. 7b). The high selectivity of the CDs(WTL) towards tetracyclines provides a potential for the sensitive determination of tetracyclines in real complex systems (environments where Hg2+ are abundant should be avoided). The reasons for fluorescence quenching of the CDs(WTL) by tetracycline may be that tetracycline can react with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of fluorescent carbon points, resulting in static quenching [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The establishment of three kinds of quantitative models based on the fluorescent system of CDs(WTL)
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the fluorescence responsive effect of the CDs(WTL) towards TC, the quenching effect has been studied under different concentration levels. As demonstrated in Fig. 8 a, the fluorescence intensity decreases regularly as TC increases from 0.00 μM to 120.0 μM, the fluorescence intensity was nearly quenched totally with TC concentration reached 120.0 μM. Interestingly, as demonstrated in Fig. 8b, the wavelength of maximum emission shifts to a longer wavelength with TC concentration increasing. However, carbon dots reported in references mostly displayed the excitation-dependent property, nearly no research mentioned the phenomenon that fluorescence spectral red-shift caused by the concentration increasing of analytes of interest in some fluorescence quenching systems [6,58]. Moreover, we selected two other batches of discarded tobacco leaves from last year and this year to synthesize carbon dots using the same synthesis method and measure the fluorescence intensity in different concentrations of tetracycline. Experiments show that the fluorescence spectrum of the CDs(WTL) and its quenching degree in different concentrations of tetracycline are not affected by the batch of raw materials (Fig. S1). Whereas, due to the existence of a slightly concentration-dependent phenomenon, it is usually difficult to acquire quite satisfactory quantitative results based on the TQ model. In this work, based on the fluorescence quenching system with a slightly spectrum shift, three kinds of quantification methods including TQ, PLS, and QFM, have been employed for quantitative analysis of TC in real samples of TC tablet and river water. As demonstrated in Table S1, 10 standard solutions with TC concentration in the range of 0.00–90.00 μM were used as calibration set for constructing quantitative model, 6 samples with TC concentration in the range of 30.00–80.00 μM were applied as validation set for verifying the accuracy of the obtained model. Then the optimized model was applied to predict the actual concentration of TC in a serial of real samples.

As demonstrated in Fig. 9 a, the coefficient of determination (R 2) of the QFM model reaches 0.9991, the validation and spiked river water samples locate well on the diagonal, which means the as-built model possesses strong prediction ability. The average recoveries for the validation set was 97.2 ± 4.4%, and the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) were 0.52 and 1.56 μM, respectively. However, for the commonly used multivariate calibration method of PLS, the obtained coefficient of determination (R
2 ) is 0.9834 (Fig. 9b), and the recovery for validation set was 90.0 ± 13.8%, respectively, did not gain satisfactory results. For low TC concentration samples, the obtained prediction results are incorrect, which makes the average recovery worse, after eliminating two low concentration samples in calibration set, the recovery for validation set increased to 95.6 ± 2.7%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of TC in tablet samples from three pharmaceutical companies
ce:section.ce:para: Three quantitative models built above were applied to predict TC in actual tablet samples obtained from three pharmaceutical companies. Table 1 summarizes the corresponding results obtained from the models built above. For tablet samples TCA01 - 03, TCB01 - 03, and TCC01 - 03, the concentrations of TC predicted by QFM were 36.75 ± 0.29, 19.36 ± 1.56, and 18.31 ± 0.40 μM, respectively. The spike recoveries were 98.9 ± 3.6%, 102.5 ± 6.2%, and 98.5 ± 2.7%, respectively. While for the results obtained from PLS, the spike recoveries for these three kinds of tablet samples were 79.5 ± 22.2%, 130.7 ± 16.9%, 70.2 ± 25.2%, and for the results obtained from TQ, the spike recoveries for these three kinds of tablet samples were 78.1 ± 5.8%, 134.5 ± 17.9% and 95.4 ± 13.2%, respectively, these results are quite unsatisfactory. Due to the red-shift of the peak emission wavelength, the unsatisfactory results of calibration and validation samples induced the unacceptable results of real tablet samples. However, QFM model can overcome these problems and get better results. As shown in Fig. 9d, the average relative predictive error (ARPE) obtained by QFM, PLS, and TQ were 2.9%, 22.0%, and 21.9%, respectively. These results of ARPE obtained from QFM are quite better than that of PLS and TQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of TC in river water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three quantitative models built above were also applied to predict TC in river water samples. Table S2 lists the corresponding results obtained from these models. The spike recoveries obtained by QFM, PLS, and TQ were 99.4 ± 5.0%, 96.4 ± 4.3%, and 108.8 ± 4.3%, respectively; the APRE obtained by QFM, PLS, and TQ (Fig. 9d) were 3.9%, 4.5%, and 8.8%, respectively. These results of QFM are also better than these of PLS and TQ.

The results obtained from HPLC method are consistent well with that of QFM method based on the fluorescence quenching system of CDs(WTL), the comparison results are demonstrated in Table 2 . The F-test and t-test were performed, for there 3 tablet samples, the calculated value of F were 6.73, 1.29, and 5.10, respectively, all far below the threshold of 39.0, which suggest that no significant difference exists between these two methods at the 5% significance level. Furthermore, two-tailed t-test results for these 3 tablet samples also verified that consistent results were obtained between HPLC and QFM. The results confirmed that combined with the QFM model, the CDs(WTL) can be used as a fluorescence probe for accurately and efficiently quantitative determination of TC in practical application.

The reproducibility of the proposed TC quantitative detection method was carried out by performing CDs(WTL)-QFM analysis on 3 TC tablet samples and river water samples in 3 consecutive days, as shown in Table S3. For 3 kinds of tablet samples, the results obtained from QFM based on fluorescence quenching system in 3 consecutive days were consistent well with each other. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for spiked river samples and actual tablet samples were all less than 2.0%, which suggest that the quantification method that based on fluorescence quenching of CDs(WTL) coupled with QFM has excellent reproducibility.

In this work, waste tobacco leaves were used as carbon source for synthesizing carbon dots through a facile, green, and eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal approach. The as-prepared CDs(WTL) possess many excellent advantages, including good water solubility, small particle size, high fluorescence quantum yield of 13.7%, and selectivity to tetracycline with fluorescence intensity decreased obviously. In addition, the CDs(WTL) were used as a fluorescence probe for the determination of tetracycline in real samples of tetracycline tablet and river water, coupled with three kinds of quantification methods of QFM, PLS, and TQ. Compared to methods of PLS and TQ, QFM displayed better performance with results consistent well with that obtained from the reference method of HPLC, the reason is that QFM can overcome the errors introduced by peak shift effect. These satisfactory results indicated that, waste tobacco leaves could be used as the carbon source for producing carbon dots, which could be utilized as a fluorescent probe for quantitative analysis. The chemometric model of QFM can be introduced to deal with the peak shift effect which frequently occurs in this fluorescence quantification field to improve the accuracy of fluorescence quantification.

Yan-Mei Liang: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hui Yang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Bo Zhou: Visualization. Yang Chen: Validation. Min Yang: Software, Writing – review & editing. Ke-Su Wei: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiu-Fang Yan: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Chao Kang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Project of Guizhou Province (grant numbers [2020]4Y133, [2019]1070, and [2020]4102), the Science and Technology Project of China National Tobacco Corporation Guizhou Company (grant numbers 201822 and 201922), and the High-Level Innovative Talents Training Program of Guizhou Province (grant number 20165663).
